bullocks-oriole.region: Native to western North America, this species breeds across the western United States and winters in Mexico and Central America.
bullocks-oriole.behavior: It weaves a hanging pendulous nest from plant fibers and forages on insects, nectar, and fruit.
bullocks-oriole.funFact: Bullock's oriole was once lumped with the Baltimore oriole as a single species called the 'northern oriole,' until genetic studies split them apart.

yellow-headed-blackbird.region: Breeds in freshwater marshes of western and central North America, wintering south into Mexico.
yellow-headed-blackbird.behavior: This species forages on the ground for seeds and insects, often in large mixed flocks on agricultural land.
yellow-headed-blackbird.funFact: The yellow-headed blackbird is the sole member of its genus — its scientific name repeats the Greek word for 'yellow head' twice: Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus.

cactus-wren.region: Endemic to the deserts of the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, where it is the state bird of Arizona.
cactus-wren.behavior: A ground forager that picks through desert debris for insects, this species rarely needs to drink, meeting its water needs entirely from its diet; it builds globe-shaped nests among spiny desert plants for protection.
cactus-wren.funFact: The cactus wren's song has been compared to a car engine that refuses to start — a harsh, repetitive churring delivered loudly from atop a saguaro.

verdin.region: Found in arid scrublands and desert washes of the southwestern United States and northern Mexico, this species is the only New World member of the penduline tit family.
verdin.behavior: It gleans insects and spiders from foliage and cacti, and will also take nectar from flowers; it builds elaborate year-round roost nests in addition to its breeding nests.
verdin.funFact: The verdin is the only North American representative of the Old World penduline tit family, Remizidae — a family otherwise confined to Europe, Asia, and Africa.

phainopepla.region: Found in desert scrub and oak woodlands of the southwestern United States and Mexico, this species is the northernmost member of the silky-flycatcher family.
phainopepla.behavior: Highly dependent on mistletoe berries, this species can consume more than a thousand a day, dispersing the seeds widely; it also sallies from perches to catch flying insects.
phainopepla.funFact: The phainopepla's name comes from the Greek for 'shining robe,' a reference to the glossy jet-black plumage of the male.

vermilion-flycatcher.region: Inhabits riparian areas, open savanna, and semi-arid scrub from the southwestern United States through South America.
vermilion-flycatcher.behavior: An aerial insectivore, it launches from exposed perches to snatch flying prey; despite being socially monogamous, it regularly engages in extra-pair mating and within-species brood parasitism.
vermilion-flycatcher.funFact: The male vermilion flycatcher is one of the most vivid birds in the Americas — his crown and underparts blaze a neon scarlet that stands out strikingly against his dark brown back and wings.

common-chaffinch.region: Breeds widely across Europe and the Palearctic east to Siberia; northern populations migrate south in winter while birds in warmer areas are sedentary.
common-chaffinch.behavior: Outside the breeding season it flocks in open fields to forage for seeds on the ground; during the breeding season it hunts caterpillars and invertebrates among tree foliage to feed its young.
common-chaffinch.funFact: The common chaffinch is one of Europe's most abundant birds, and the male's bright blue-grey cap and rust-red breast make him unmistakeable singing from an exposed perch.

european-greenfinch.region: Widespread across Europe, North Africa, and Southwest Asia, with introduced populations now established in Australia, New Zealand, and South America.
european-greenfinch.behavior: A seed specialist, it uses its stout bill to crack open hard seed cases, and visits garden feeders readily; some northern populations make short-distance migrations south in winter.
european-greenfinch.funFact: The European greenfinch's chunky bill gives it enough force to crack open the hardest seed cases, allowing it to exploit food sources inaccessible to smaller finches.

eurasian-bullfinch.region: Resident across much of Europe and northern Asia, favouring woodland edges, hedgerows, and orchards.
eurasian-bullfinch.behavior: This species feeds predominantly on buds, seeds, and berries; in spring it can devastate orchards by stripping fruit-tree buds before they open, earning it a long history of persecution by growers.
eurasian-bullfinch.funFact: The Eurasian bullfinch was so notorious for raiding orchard buds that in 16th-century England a bounty was placed on every bird killed — a penny per head.

common-linnet.region: Found across Europe, western Asia, and North Africa in open farmland, heathland, and scrubby hillsides; northern populations migrate south in winter.
common-linnet.behavior: A gregarious seed-eater that forages in flocks on open ground, favouring the seeds of weeds and grasses; it derives its very name from its fondness for flax and hemp seeds.
common-linnet.funFact: The common linnet takes both its English name and its genus name Linaria from flax — the plant from which linen is made — reflecting its historical fondness for flax seeds.

eurasian-siskin.region: Very common throughout Europe and Eurasia, favouring forested areas of coniferous and mixed woodland; in some years populations make large irruptive southward migrations in winter.
eurasian-siskin.behavior: An acrobatic feeder, it hangs upside-down like a tit to pick seeds from alder catkins and conifer cones; it is a sociable bird that visits garden feeding stations readily.
eurasian-siskin.funFact: The Eurasian siskin periodically migrates in massive irruptive waves every few years — the triggers are poorly understood but are thought to relate to fluctuating food supply and climate.

hawfinch.region: Breeds across Europe and temperate Asia, favouring deciduous and mixed woodland with large trees, especially hornbeam; many Asian birds migrate south in winter.
hawfinch.behavior: A powerful seed-cracker, it uses its massive bill to split open cherry stones and other hard fruit kernels that would defeat smaller finches; it is usually seen in pairs or small groups and is notably unobtrusive.
hawfinch.funFact: The hawfinch can exert a bite force of up to 50 kg — powerful enough to crack open olive stones and cherry pits with a bill that looks almost comically oversized for its body.

yellowhammer.region: Native to open farmland, hedgerows, and scrubby hillsides across Eurasia; most European birds are year-round residents, though eastern populations migrate south in winter.
yellowhammer.behavior: Primarily a ground-feeding seed-eater, supplementing its diet with invertebrates during the breeding season; it forms small flocks in winter and sings persistently from exposed perches.
yellowhammer.funFact: The yellowhammer's song — traditionally rendered 'a little bit of bread and no cheese' — inspired Beethoven, who reportedly used its rhythm in his Fifth Symphony.

common-reed-bunting.region: Breeds throughout Europe and much of the Palearctic in wetland margins and marsh vegetation, damp moorland, and rush pastures; while many birds migrate south in winter, those in milder western areas remain year-round.
common-reed-bunting.behavior: This species forages on seeds and invertebrates in wet vegetation; males sing persistently from prominent wetland perches during the breeding season.
common-reed-bunting.funFact: Though strongly associated with wetlands, the common reed bunting has increasingly colonised dry arable farmland, showing a surprising adaptability to changing agricultural landscapes.

eurasian-tree-sparrow.region: Breeds across temperate Eurasia and Southeast Asia, inhabiting lightly wooded open countryside and farmland; in Europe it avoids urban centres, which are dominated by the house sparrow.
eurasian-tree-sparrow.behavior: Mainly a seed-eater but takes invertebrates during the breeding season; it nests in natural cavities, holes in buildings, and disused nests of other species.
eurasian-tree-sparrow.funFact: Unlike the house sparrow, where males and females look different, both sexes of the Eurasian tree sparrow wear the same rich chestnut cap and neat black cheek patch.

coal-tit.region: A widespread and common resident of forests throughout the temperate to subtropical Palearctic, including North Africa, with a particular affinity for coniferous woodland.
coal-tit.behavior: An energetic forager that gleans insects and seeds from foliage, it has the habit of caching food items to retrieve in leaner times, and readily visits garden feeders.
coal-tit.funFact: Despite being one of Europe's smallest tits, the coal tit is a bold hoarder — it can cache thousands of seeds per winter, remembering the locations with remarkable precision.

long-tailed-tit.region: Found throughout Eurasia in deciduous and mixed woodland, scrub, heathland, farmland, parks, and gardens; it is largely resident across its range.
long-tailed-tit.behavior: An insectivore that gleans tiny invertebrates from foliage, it travels in tight, noisy family flocks of six to seventeen individuals outside the breeding season and roosts huddled together for warmth.
long-tailed-tit.funFact: The long-tailed tit's nest is an engineering marvel — an oval ball of moss, lichen, and spiders' webs lined with up to 2,000 feathers, which stretches to accommodate the growing chicks.

dunnock.region: Found throughout temperate Europe and into Asian Russia in hedgerows, woodland edges, gardens, and scrub; it has also been introduced to New Zealand.
dunnock.behavior: A quiet, unobtrusive bird that shuffles along the ground picking up small invertebrates and seeds with a characteristic creeping shuffle; it has one of the most complex mating systems of any European songbird.
dunnock.funFact: The dunnock has one of the most extraordinary sex lives in European birds — females routinely mate with multiple males simultaneously, and males will peck at the female's cloaca to remove a rival's sperm before mating.

song-thrush.region: Breeds across the West Palearctic in forests, gardens, and parks; it is partially migratory, with many individuals wintering in southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.
song-thrush.behavior: An omnivore that famously uses a favourite stone as an 'anvil' to smash open snail shells; it also eats earthworms, insects, and berries.
song-thrush.funFact: The song thrush's distinctive song repeats each musical phrase two or three times — a habit so characteristic that the poet Robert Browning wrote 'That's the wise thrush; he sings each song twice over.'

mistle-thrush.region: Common across much of Europe, temperate Asia, and North Africa in open woods, parks, hedges, and cultivated land; northern populations migrate south in winter.
mistle-thrush.behavior: A bold, pugnacious feeder on a wide variety of invertebrates, seeds, and berries; in winter it will aggressively defend a single mistletoe clump or berry-bearing tree against all comers, chasing off rival birds.
mistle-thrush.funFact: The mistle thrush earned the old name 'stormcock' for its habit of singing loudly from treetops even in driving rain and wind — behaviour most birds wisely avoid.

redwing.region: A native of Europe and the Palearctic, this small member of the thrush family breeds in northern forests and migrates south across Europe in winter.
redwing.behavior: It feeds on berries, fallen fruit, and earthworms, often forming mixed flocks with fieldfares during winter movements across open fields and hedgerows.
redwing.funFact: The redwing is Britain's smallest true thrush and gets its name from the vivid rusty-orange patch on its flanks and underwings, visible in flight.

fieldfare.region: Breeds in woodland and scrub across northern Europe and the Palearctic; strongly migratory, it winters in large numbers in southern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.
fieldfare.behavior: Omnivorous across the seasons — eating molluscs, insects, and earthworms in summer and switching to berries, grain, and seeds in winter; migrating flocks often travel in the company of other winter thrushes.
fieldfare.funFact: Fieldfares defend their nesting colonies with a unique weapon: they mob predators by dive-bombing them and defecating on them, the foul coating matting feathers and sometimes killing small hawks.

european-pied-flycatcher.region: Breeds across most of Europe and the western Palearctic, nesting typically in oak woodland; it is fully migratory, wintering in tropical Africa.
european-pied-flycatcher.behavior: Mainly insectivorous, catching caterpillars, flies, spiders, and ants; males practice polygyny, travelling large distances to acquire a second mate while still assisting with chick-rearing at the first nest.
european-pied-flycatcher.funFact: Male European pied flycatchers are serial bigamists — they attract a second female at a distant nest site, often concealing their paired status by adopting a duller plumage near the second female.

common-nightingale.region: Breeds in woodland, scrub, and dense thickets across Europe and western Asia; it migrates to sub-Saharan Africa for the winter.
common-nightingale.behavior: A mostly terrestrial species that forages on the ground for insects and invertebrates; it is best known for its song, which is delivered by the male both day and night during the breeding season.
common-nightingale.funFact: The common nightingale's song is considered one of the most beautiful in the bird world — so complex and powerful that it can be heard clearly above the ambient noise of a city at night.

black-redstart.region: A widespread breeder in rocky habitats, cliffs, and urban areas across Eurasia and North Africa, from the British Isles east to central China; northern and eastern populations migrate south in winter.
black-redstart.behavior: This small, terrestrial species forages on the ground and among rocks for insects and invertebrates, frequently quivering its rusty tail; it is strongly associated with built-up areas and rocky landscapes.
black-redstart.funFact: During World War II, the black redstart colonised bombed-out rubble sites in British cities — the first records of breeding in England were on London's devastated bomb sites in the 1940s.

common-redstart.region: Breeds across much of Europe and western Asia in open woodland, parks, and gardens; it is fully migratory, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa.
common-redstart.behavior: An insectivorous species that flits between low perches and the ground to catch invertebrates; it is a hole-nester and readily takes to nest boxes.
common-redstart.funFact: The common redstart's name comes from the Old English word 'steort' meaning tail — a reference to its constantly quivering, vivid orange-red tail that remains its most recognisable feature.

european-stonechat.region: Found across Europe as far east as Ukraine and the South Caucasus, and in parts of North Africa, in open heathland, gorse scrub, and coastal grassland.
european-stonechat.behavior: This species perches conspicuously on the tops of bushes and fence posts, dropping to the ground to take insects and other small invertebrates; its alarm call sounds like two pebbles being knocked together.
european-stonechat.funFact: The European stonechat gets its name from its sharp, pebble-clacking alarm call — a distinctive 'wheet-chak-chak' that carries clearly across open heathland.

northern-wheatear.region: Breeds in open stony country across Europe, Asia, and has footholds in northeastern Canada, Greenland, Alaska, and northwestern Canada; all populations winter in Africa.
northern-wheatear.behavior: An insectivorous species that forages on the ground, darting after prey with quick runs and dips; it nests in rock crevices and rabbit burrows.
northern-wheatear.funFact: The northern wheatear undertakes one of the most impressive migrations of any small bird — Alaskan-breeding birds fly more than 14,000 km across Siberia and the Middle East to reach Africa.

common-house-martin.region: Breeds in Europe, North Africa, and west-central Asia; it is fully migratory, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa and southwestern Asia.
common-house-martin.behavior: An aerial insectivore that feeds entirely on flying insects caught in flight; it builds distinctive closed cup nests from mud pellets attached under eaves of buildings, usually in loose colonies.
common-house-martin.funFact: The common house martin builds its nest entirely from mud pellets — a single nest may require more than a thousand trips to a muddy puddle to collect enough material.

sand-martin.region: Has one of the widest breeding ranges of any swallow-family bird, spanning the entire Holarctic — from Europe across Asia to the Pacific, and throughout North America; it winters in Africa and South America.
sand-martin.behavior: An aerial insectivore, it hunts flying insects over water and open ground; unlike most swallow-family birds, it excavates long burrow tunnels in sandy river banks and cliffs to nest in colonies.
sand-martin.funFact: The sand martin is one of the first summer migrants to arrive in Europe each spring — its appearance at river banks in February or March is a classic early sign that winter is ending.

common-swift.region: Breeds across Europe and Asia, migrating to sub-Saharan Africa; it is found in open skies above towns, cities, and countryside wherever flying insects are abundant.
common-swift.behavior: Almost entirely aerial — it eats, sleeps, and even mates on the wing; its tiny, weak legs are used only to cling to vertical surfaces, and non-breeding individuals may remain airborne for up to ten months continuously.
common-swift.funFact: The common swift can stay airborne for up to ten months without landing — eating, sleeping, and even mating in the air, making it one of the most aerial vertebrates on Earth.

white-wagtail.region: Breeds across the Palearctic from Europe through Asia, and into western Alaska; most populations migrate to Africa for the winter, though birds in milder areas are resident.
white-wagtail.behavior: An insectivorous bird of open, bare ground near water and human habitation; it forages actively on foot, bobbing its tail continuously as it pursues prey across car parks, riverbanks, and paved areas.
white-wagtail.funFact: The white wagtail is the national bird of Latvia and is one of Europe's most familiar birds — its constantly bobbing tail and bold black-and-white pattern make it instantly recognisable.

grey-wagtail.region: Widely distributed across Eurasia, with several populations that breed in Europe and northern Asia migrating to tropical regions in Africa and Asia; it is always associated with running water when breeding.
grey-wagtail.behavior: This species forages along fast-flowing streams and rivers for aquatic insects, frequently wagging its long tail and flying low with bounding undulations; outside the breeding season it may also be found at lakes and coasts.
grey-wagtail.funFact: Despite its name, the grey wagtail is one of Europe's most colourful waterside birds — breeding males have a bold black throat and vivid sulphur-yellow underparts that outshine many species with 'yellow' in their name.

yellow-wagtail.region: Breeds across much of temperate Europe and Asia in open wet meadows and grassland near water; most populations migrate to tropical Africa and southern Asia for the winter.
yellow-wagtail.behavior: An insectivorous ground forager, it walks through low vegetation picking up flies, beetles, and other invertebrates, often following livestock to catch insects disturbed by their hooves.
yellow-wagtail.funFact: The western yellow wagtail has an extraordinary number of subspecies — males across its range show at least eight distinct head-colour patterns, from yellow to blue-grey to black, leading to endless taxonomic debate.

meadow-pipit.region: Breeds throughout much of the Palearctic, from Greenland and Iceland east to the Ural Mountains; it migrates to southern Europe, North Africa, and southwestern Asia in winter, though western populations are largely resident.
meadow-pipit.behavior: A ground-nesting, ground-foraging species of open moorland and grassland, picking up small insects and seeds; it is a key host species for the common cuckoo across much of its range.
meadow-pipit.funFact: The meadow pipit is the cuckoo's most important host across upland Britain — female cuckoos that parasitise meadow pipits lay eggs closely matching the pipit's own speckled pattern.

eurasian-skylark.region: Widespread across Europe and the Palearctic, with introduced populations in Australia, New Zealand, Canada, and Hawaii; it is a bird of open farmland and heath.
eurasian-skylark.behavior: Males deliver their celebrated song in prolonged hovering flight at heights of 50–100 metres above ground; the species forages on the ground for seeds and insects, and the female nests in a shallow ground depression far from any tree.
eurasian-skylark.funFact: A male Eurasian skylark may sing continuously for more than an hour while hovering at 100 metres — the song contains a complex stream of trills and mimicry delivered without pause.

common-starling.region: Native to temperate Europe and across the Palearctic to western Mongolia; it has been introduced as an invasive species to North America, Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa, among others.
common-starling.behavior: An omnivore taking a wide range of invertebrates, seeds, and fruit; it is famous for forming enormous roosting flocks — called murmurations — that perform sweeping, co-ordinated aerial displays before settling to roost.
common-starling.funFact: The common starling's gift for mimicry is so impressive that Pliny the Elder documented it, Shakespeare wrote about it, and Mozart famously kept one as a pet — it learned to sing a variation of his Piano Concerto No. 17.

eurasian-magpie.region: A resident breeding bird throughout the northern Eurasian continent, found in farmland, woodland edges, parks, and gardens.
eurasian-magpie.behavior: An omnivorous and opportunistic corvid, it eats insects, small vertebrates, carrion, grain, and fruit; it builds large domed stick nests and is known for its inquisitive, bold behaviour around humans.
eurasian-magpie.funFact: The Eurasian magpie is one of the few non-mammal species to pass the mirror self-recognition test, placing it in the same cognitive category as great apes, elephants, and dolphins.

western-jackdaw.region: Found across Europe, western Asia, and western North Africa in farmland, open woodland, coastal cliffs, and urban settings; mostly resident, though northern and eastern birds migrate south in winter.
western-jackdaw.behavior: An omnivorous and opportunistic feeder consuming plant material, invertebrates, and urban food waste; it lives in small groups with a complex social structure and nests in cavities in trees, cliffs, or buildings.
western-jackdaw.funFact: The western jackdaw has piercing pale eyes unlike any other crow, giving it an almost otherworldly stare — the pale iris is unique among European corvids and intensifies its reputation for intelligence.

carrion-crow.region: Native to western Europe and the eastern Palearctic, occupying a wide range of open and semi-open habitats including farmland, woodland, coasts, and urban areas.
carrion-crow.behavior: A highly adaptable, all-black omnivore that eats almost anything — invertebrates, small vertebrates, eggs, grain, carrion, and food waste; it is a solitary nester but may gather in communal roosts outside the breeding season.
carrion-crow.funFact: Carrion crows have been filmed dropping hard-shelled walnuts onto pedestrian crossings, then waiting for cars to crack them open before flying down to collect the kernel on the red light.

common-wood-pigeon.region: Native to the western Palearctic and the largest of the European pigeons, it is found in woodland, farmland, parks, and suburban gardens across Europe and into western Asia.
common-wood-pigeon.behavior: A largely vegetarian species with a flexible diet centred on plant material including cereals, clover, and seeds; it feeds mainly on the ground or in trees and is considered an agricultural pest in many areas.
common-wood-pigeon.funFact: The common wood pigeon is Europe's largest native pigeon and has boomed in numbers as an arable-farming specialist — in Britain alone the population now exceeds five million pairs.

eurasian-collared-dove.region: Native to Europe, Asia, and northern Africa, this species has also been introduced to Japan, North and Central America, and the Caribbean; it is strongly associated with human settlement.
eurasian-collared-dove.behavior: A seed and grain feeder, it is highly adaptable to urban and suburban environments and nests year-round in trees and hedges near buildings; it is one of the most rapidly expanding birds of the 20th century.
eurasian-collared-dove.funFact: The Eurasian collared dove's expansion across Europe in the 20th century was one of ornithology's great spectacles — it spread from Turkey to Britain in just 20 years, reaching Ireland by 1959.

european-turtle-dove.region: Breeds across the southwestern Palearctic including North Africa, migrating south to winter in northern sub-Saharan Africa.
european-turtle-dove.behavior: A seed-eater of open farmland and woodland edges, it is a long-distance migrant that has declined catastrophically across Europe due to habitat loss and hunting pressure on migration.
european-turtle-dove.funFact: The European turtle dove is now one of Europe's most threatened migratory birds — its population has fallen by more than 90% since the 1970s, and it has virtually disappeared from much of northern Europe.

common-cuckoo.region: A widespread summer migrant to Europe and Asia, wintering in Africa; it occupies a wide range of habitats including woodland, moorland, and reedbeds wherever suitable host species nest.
common-cuckoo.behavior: A brood parasite that lays its eggs in the nests of other species — particularly dunnocks, meadow pipits, and reed warblers — with each female specialising on a single host species and laying matching eggs; the adult female also mimics a sparrowhawk in flight to scare hosts away from their nests.
common-cuckoo.funFact: A cuckoo chick, blind and naked just hours after hatching, instinctively heaves all other eggs and chicks out of the nest — ensuring it alone receives all the foster parents' food.

great-spotted-woodpecker.region: Found across the Palearctic including parts of North Africa, inhabiting all types of woodland; largely resident across most of its range, though some northern birds make movements when conifer crops fail.
great-spotted-woodpecker.behavior: A versatile forager that chisels into bark for insect larvae, extracts seeds from pine cones, and will raid the nest holes of other birds for eggs and chicks; it also drums loudly on resonant branches for territorial advertisement.
great-spotted-woodpecker.funFact: The great spotted woodpecker can strike a tree at up to 20 times per second — its brain is protected by shock-absorbing bone structure and a thick, spongy skull that cushions each impact.

european-green-woodpecker.region: Resident across much of Europe and the western Palearctic, absent from Spain and Portugal where it is replaced by the closely related Iberian green woodpecker.
european-green-woodpecker.behavior: Unusually for its family, this species spends most of its time foraging on the ground for ants, using its long sticky tongue to probe anthills; it rarely drums on trees and is more often heard by its loud, laughing call than seen.
european-green-woodpecker.funFact: The European green woodpecker's laugh-like call — the 'yaffle' — gave it one of its folk names, and it was loud enough to inspire a BBC animated character of the same name.

eurasian-wryneck.region: Breeds in temperate Europe and Asia in open countryside, woodland, and orchards; most populations migrate to tropical Africa and southern Asia for the winter, though some are resident in northwest Africa.
eurasian-wryneck.behavior: Its chief prey is ants and other insects, which it finds in decaying wood or on the ground using a long, sticky tongue; when threatened at the nest, it twists and cranes its head in a slow snake-like display while hissing.
eurasian-wryneck.funFact: The Eurasian wryneck can rotate its head almost 180 degrees — a startling trick that earned it the name 'snake bird' in several languages and was once thought to be a sign of witchcraft.

european-bee-eater.region: Breeds in southern and central Europe, northern and southern Africa, and western Asia; most populations are strongly migratory, wintering in tropical Africa.
european-bee-eater.behavior: A specialist predator of flying insects — particularly bees, wasps, and hornets — which it catches in aerial sallies; before swallowing a stinging insect, it beats it against a perch and wipes off the venom.
european-bee-eater.funFact: The European bee-eater is immune neither to bee venom nor to stings — instead it methodically rubs each captured bee against a branch to rupture the venom sac and expel the sting before eating it.

european-roller.region: Breeds across Europe, the Maghreb, West Asia, and Central Asia; it is strongly migratory, wintering in the dry wooded savanna and bushy plains of southern Africa.
european-roller.behavior: A perch-hunting insectivore, it watches from exposed vantage points for large insects, lizards, and small vertebrates, then drops to seize them on the ground; it nests in tree holes and is not closely related to any other European bird.
european-roller.funFact: The European roller gets its name from its spectacular courtship display — the male performs wild rolling and tumbling dives, twisting and somersaulting through the air while calling loudly.

eurasian-hoopoe.region: Native across Europe, Asia, and Africa; it is migratory in the northern part of its range, wintering south of the breeding areas in sub-Saharan Africa and tropical Asia.
eurasian-hoopoe.behavior: This species probes soil and leaf litter for insects, larvae, and small reptiles with its long, curved bill; it is most active in open ground including lawns, pastures, and vineyards, and nests in natural cavities.
eurasian-hoopoe.funFact: The Eurasian hoopoe was chosen as Israel's national bird in 2008, and its elaborate fan-shaped crest — which it raises in alarm — has made it one of the most visually iconic birds across three continents.

common-pheasant.region: Native to Asia and the extreme southeast of Europe, but has been widely introduced across Europe, North America, and beyond as a game bird; it occupies woodland edges, farmland, and scrubby grassland.
common-pheasant.behavior: Largely ground-dwelling, it forages for seeds, berries, insects, and small vertebrates; males are polygynous, maintaining harems of several females and defending territories with loud, crowing calls.
common-pheasant.funFact: The common pheasant is probably the world's most widely introduced game bird — more than 50 million are released annually in Britain alone, where the species is not naturally native.

grey-partridge.region: Found across Europe and into western Asia in open arable farmland, grassland, and heath; it is largely sedentary within its range.
grey-partridge.behavior: A ground-dwelling species that forages for seeds, leaves, and insects, often in family groups known as coveys; it nests in dense vegetation on the ground and is particularly dependent on invertebrate-rich field margins for chick survival.
grey-partridge.funFact: The grey partridge is one of Europe's most farmland-dependent birds and has declined by more than 85% in Britain since the 1970s — a direct consequence of herbicide use eliminating the insect-rich weedy margins chicks need.

red-legged-partridge.region: Native to southwestern Europe — primarily France, Spain, and Portugal — where it inhabits open rocky hillsides, scrubland, and arable farmland; introduced populations exist in Britain and elsewhere.
red-legged-partridge.behavior: A ground-dweller that prefers to run rather than fly when disturbed; it is a seed-eater, though young birds depend heavily on insects for protein; its call is a distinctive three-syllable 'ka-chu-chu'.
red-legged-partridge.funFact: The red-legged partridge has an unusual family arrangement — females sometimes lay two separate clutches simultaneously, leaving one for the male to incubate while she incubates the other herself.

common-quail.region: Breeds in the western Palearctic and across Europe and North Africa; it is mainly migratory, wintering in Africa and southern India.
common-quail.behavior: A secretive ground-nester that is far more often heard than seen; it forages on seeds and small invertebrates among crops and grassland, and its characteristic three-note call carries surprisingly far across open fields.
common-quail.funFact: The common quail is Europe's only fully migratory galliform — all other members of the grouse, partridge, and pheasant family in Europe are largely sedentary, making it a remarkable exception.

western-capercaillie.region: Found across Europe and the Palearctic in dense boreal and montane forests; populations in central Europe are fragmented and declining, while the northern range remains more stable.
western-capercaillie.behavior: Primarily a ground-dwelling forest bird, it feeds on pine needles, berries, and plant shoots; males perform spectacular competitive courtship displays at traditional gathering sites called leks, where they fan their tails, puff their chests, and produce extraordinary gurgling and popping calls.
western-capercaillie.funFact: The western capercaillie is the world's largest grouse — a male can weigh up to 6 kg — and during its lek display it becomes so absorbed that it is temporarily deaf to approaching predators.

eurasian-woodcock.region: Found in temperate and subarctic Eurasia in moist woodland with soft ground for probing; birds from northern and eastern areas migrate south and west in winter.
eurasian-woodcock.behavior: A cryptically camouflaged species that probes moist soil with its long, sensitive bill for earthworms and invertebrates; its eyes are set so far back on its head that it has nearly 360-degree vision, allowing it to watch for predators while feeding.
eurasian-woodcock.funFact: The Eurasian woodcock's eyes are positioned so far back on its skull that it has a blind spot directly in front of its bill — but can see almost fully behind and above itself without moving its head.

northern-lapwing.region: Common through temperate Eurosiberia in open farmland, grassland, and wetland margins; it is partially migratory, with northern populations moving south in winter.
northern-lapwing.behavior: This species nests on the ground in open fields, using a tumbling, acrobatic display flight and loud calls to distract and mob predators; it forages by sight for earthworms and insects on short grassland.
northern-lapwing.funFact: The northern lapwing's broad, rounded wings create a distinctive 'floppy' wingbeat and a characteristic whistling sound in flight — the source of its common name 'peewit', which imitates both the wing noise and its call.

european-golden-plover.region: Breeds on upland moorland and tundra across northern Europe and Iceland; it is migratory, wintering in lower-lying grassland and farmland across western and southern Europe and North Africa.
european-golden-plover.behavior: A ground-nesting species that forages by sight for earthworms, beetles, and other invertebrates on short turf; it forms large winter flocks, often mixing with northern lapwings on lowland pasture.
european-golden-plover.funFact: The European golden plover has the most northerly breeding range of any European plover, nesting on bleak upland bogs — and its bubbling, mournful call is one of the iconic sounds of a British moorland spring.

common-redshank.region: A Eurasian wading bird breeding across a wide range from western Europe to eastern Asia in coastal marshes, damp meadows, and moorland; it is partially migratory, wintering on coasts and estuaries.
common-redshank.behavior: This species forages by sight and touch in mud and shallow water for invertebrates, worms, and small fish; it is an extremely alert and noisy species, often the first to take alarm and call loudly when a predator approaches, earning it the name 'sentinel of the marshes'.
common-redshank.funFact: The common redshank is known as the 'sentinel of the marshes' — its piercing alarm calls are so reliable that other wading birds use it as a watchman, taking flight whenever it calls.

black-tailed-godwit.region: Breeds across a wide range from Iceland through Europe and central Asia in fens, damp meadows, moorland, and bogs; it winters on estuaries, swamps, and flooded fields across the Indian subcontinent, Australia, western Europe, and West Africa.
black-tailed-godwit.behavior: A long-billed wader that probes deeply into mud and soft soil for invertebrates, worms, and plant material; it is more likely to feed inland and on freshwater than its close relative the bar-tailed godwit.
black-tailed-godwit.funFact: The black-tailed godwit is classified as Near Threatened globally — its Icelandic population, which winters in the UK and Ireland, has declined sharply due to changes in the wet grassland farming it depends on for breeding.
